name: Republique
description: Beautiful French bistro with an iconic pastry case. Perfect for brunch or dinner. Don't miss the croissants!
address: 624 S La Brea Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90036

name: Guelaguetza
description: Authentic Oaxacan cuisine with incredible mole. A Koreatown institution that's worth the trip.
address: 3014 W Olympic Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90006

name: n/naka
description: Two-Michelin-star kaiseki experience. Reservations required months in advance. Worth every penny.
address: 3455 S Overland Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90034

name: Bestia
description: Industrial-chic Arts District gem. House-made charcuterie and pizza are must-tries. Book ahead!

name: Jitlada
description: Legendary Thai Town spot with Southern Thai specialties. Spice levels are no joke. Cash only!
address: 5233 W Sunset Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90027

name: Sqirl
description: Instagram-famous brunch spot. House-made jams and creative seasonal dishes. Expect a wait!
address: 720 N Virgil Ave #4, Los Angeles, CA 90029

name: HomeState - Highland Park
description: Texas-style breakfast tacos and queso. Multiple locations but Los Feliz is our favorite.
address: 5611 N Figueroa St #1, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Blue Bottle Coffee - Arts District
description: Sleek cafe with expertly crafted coffee. Great spot for laptop work or people watching.

name: Intelligentsia Coffee - Silver Lake
description: Hip coffee roaster with excellent pour-overs. The patio is perfect for sunny LA mornings.
address: 3922 W Sunset Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90029

name: Verve Coffee Roasters
description: Santa Cruz-based roaster with beautiful Venice Beach location. Amazing cortados.

name: The Virgil
description: Intimate Silver Lake bar with live music and DJs. Great cocktails and chill vibe.
address: 4519 Santa Monica Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90029

name: Bar Bandini
description: Beautiful rooftop bar in Echo Park. Perfect for sunset drinks and Italian-inspired snacks.
address: 2281 W Sunset Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90026

name: The Normandie Club
description: Sophisticated cocktail bar in Koreatown. Dark, moody atmosphere with expertly crafted drinks.

name: Potted
description: Gorgeous plant shop and pottery studio. Unique selection of cacti, succulents, and tropical plants.
address: 3158 Los Feliz Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90039

name: The Sill
description: Modern plant shop with beginner-friendly options. Great for first-time plant parents.

name: Rolling Greens
description: Gorgeous nursery and home goods shop. Amazing selection of outdoor plants and garden decor.

name: Amoeba Music
description: Legendary independent record store. Massive vinyl selection spanning all genres. A must-visit.

name: Permanent Records
description: Curated vinyl shop in Echo Park. Great for discovering indie, punk, and experimental music.

name: Mae Malai Thai House of Noodles
description: Michelin Bib Gourmand. Authentic Thai boat noodles and family recipes passed down for 60+ years.

name: Majordomo
description: David Chang's LA flagship. Famous for whole plate short ribs and Korean-inflected California cuisine.

name: Girl & the Goat
description: Top Chef Stephanie Izard's Arts District spot. Famous green beans, goat curry, and creative sharing plates.

name: Nobu Malibu
description: Iconic oceanfront sushi spot. Signature black cod miso and yellowtail jalapeño with stunning Pacific views.

name: Connie & Ted's
description: West Hollywood seafood spot inspired by classic coastal fish houses; known for oysters and lobster rolls.
address: 8171 Santa Monica Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90046

name: Redbird
description: DTLA modern American restaurant in a historic rectory space with indoor/outdoor dining and a strong cocktail program.

name: DAMA
description: Fashion District Latin-inspired restaurant + bar with a tropical patio and cocktail-forward program.

name: Citrin
description: Chef Josiah Citrin’s modern Californian dining in Santa Monica (à la carte + prix fixe).

name: kodō
description: Arts District Japanese steakhouse/izakaya-style spot with binchotan grilling and sushi offerings.
address: 710 S Santa Fe Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90021

name: Budonoki
description: Virgil Village neighborhood izakaya with sushi and Japanese drinking-food staples in a lively room.
address: 654 N Virgil Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90004

name: Perle Restaurant
description: Old Town Pasadena French-inspired, family-run restaurant focused on seasonal, sustainable ingredients.

name: Ardor
description: West Hollywood EDITION’s signature restaurant featuring live-fire cooking and seasonal California-forward menus.
address: 9040 W Sunset Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90069

name: Paradise Cove Beach Cafe
description: Classic Malibu beachfront cafe at Paradise Cove—casual seafood and coastal plates right on the sand.

name: Barra Santos
description: A tiny Portuguese restaurant in cypress park helmed by Mike Santos with food brought to you by Melissa López

name: Holbox
description: Eatery offering raw and cooked Yucatan-style seafood, served as ceviche or tacos. Located inside Mercado Paloma
address: 3655 S Grand Ave c9, Los Angeles, CA 90007

name: Ammatoli
description: Polished restaurant specializing in Mediterranean cuisine, including seafood dishes & baklava.

name: Chaak Kitchen
description: Happening restaurant specializing in modern Mexican cuisine, plus drinks & brunch.

name: Chiang Rai
description: Casual restaurant serving familiar Thai dishes such as yellow curry, pad see ew, and soft-shell crab.
address: 3832 E Anaheim St, Long Beach, CA 90804

name: Chiang Rai
description: Casual restaurant serving familiar Thai dishes such as yellow curry, pad see ew, and soft-shell crab.

name: Den Cafe
description: Coffee shop with a bohemian vibe serving pastry, soup, sandwiches & burgers off the grill.
address: 125 N Broadway D, Santa Ana, CA 92701

name: Two Hommés
description: Trendy restaurant serving Afro-centric fare such as jollof, lamb chops, and garlic noodles, plus cocktails.
address: 902 N La Brea Ave, Inglewood, CA 90302

name: Mellowist
description: Explore an ethically sourced collection of ceramics, apparel, accessories, home & garden essentials, and more.

name: The Potting Shed by Carlisle
description: Garden center supplying a range of plants, plus outdoor furniture, gifts, and antiques.

name: Grim Garden Coffee & Plants
description: Plant-filled shop offering hot drinks and sweet treats, plus exotic varieties.

name: M & M Nursery
description: Family-owned center since 1956 with fairy gardens & houses, plus cottage flowers & miniature plants.

name: Trendy Flower Shop
description: We are especially known for our Iarge variety of Indoor Plants and Special Occasion Floral Arrangements!

name: Analog Records
description: Indie venue selling new and used records, stereo gear and vintage electronics.

name: Port of Sound Record Shoppe
description: Record shop stocking a large selection of new and used vinyl, including rare finds.

name: Blind Dog HiFi and Records
description: Legendary independent record store. Massive vinyl selection spanning all genres. A must-visit.
address: 2750 Harbor Blvd b03, Costa Mesa, CA 92626

name: Factory Records
description: From the mind behind Costa Mesa's legendary Noise Nose Noise Records, Factory Records stocks thousands of record albums, 12s singles, and 45s, plus CDs. cassettes, stickers, buttons, patches,and more.

name: Vinyl Solution Records
description: Long-running store selling and trading new and used LPs, 7-inches, CDs, and cassettes, plus T-shirts.

name: Moongoat - Greenhouse
description: Make Coffee. Better. Easier. Daily We are on a simple mission. Wherever we can we are making days easier, better, all with coffee!
address: 3210 Park Center Drive, Costa Mesa, CA, 92626

name: Akashiro Nikkei Sushi Peruvian
description: Casual spot for Japanese fare, including sushi and parmesan scallops, as well as ceviche.

name: Komal
description: We bring the bold, soulful flavors of Mexico City's street food to the heart of Los Angeles, all crafted with Mexican heirloom corn. Located inside Mercado Paloma
address: 3655 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90007

name: Arroyo Records
description: New and used records in a range of genres are sold at this unpretentious store with a listening station.

name: Leaf and Spine LLC
description: Known for its diverse selection of healthy, unique, and rare houseplants, succulents, and cacti, run by knowledgeable owners Dustin Bulaon and Ernestine Segura who provide expert care advice and foster a strong community connection through their store.

name: Kiez Küche & Beer Garden
description: Kiez Küche & Beer Garden, Highland Park's newest destination for authentic German flavors and a lively beer garden experience.

name: Fat of the Land
description: European Food, Wine and Beer Magic!
address: 700 N Main St Unit A, Santa Ana, CA 92701

name: Green Cheek Beer Company
description: "Hi, we’re Green Cheek Beer Company! Just a small, independent brewery that is owned and operated by two friends who really love beer and the community it creates. DRINK WHILE SMILING."
address: 2294 N Batavia St STE C, Orange, CA 92865

name: Creature’s Plants and Coffee
description: They have a wide variety of coffee, tea and pastries on their menu. Furthermore, what set this coffee shop apart is their plant nursery and huge outdoor space.
address: 4958 Eagle Rock Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90041

name: Cosmic Vinyl
description: Very fun, eclectic gem in Eagle Rock. The store is a beautiful space, nice clean curated selection of vinyls.
address: 4868 N Eagle Rock Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90041

name: Capri Club
description: Casual bar offering cocktails and snacks for aperitivo hour, plus plates of Italian fare.
address: 4604 Eagle Rock Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90041

name: Walt’s Bar
description: Eagle Rock natives dig the retro feel of this arcade bar in a former plumber’s shop.
address: 4680 Eagle Rock Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90041

name: Verdugo Bar
description: Verdugo Bar features a stellar craft beer line-up of 20 taps in addition to a full bar with an extensive collection of spirits, specialty house-infused cocktail menu, house-crafted cocktails on draft, and boozed-up slushies.

name: The Grant
description: Neighborhood cocktail lounge offering beer, wine, and happy hours.
address: 3515 Eagle Rock Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90065

name: The Fable
description: Craft beer & drinks in a wood-paneled setting with a fireplace, also offering live music & karaoke.

name: Aunt Yvette’s Kitchen
description: Cozy eatery for Ethiopian classics like doro wat and veggie platters plus a vegan menu.

name: Kitchen Mouse
description: Vegetarian eatery offering health-focused fare in a basic, counter-serve setting.
address: 5904 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Highland Park Bowl
description: Hip, antique-filled spot for bowling, live music, craft cocktails, pizzas, salads & seasonal fare.
address: 5621 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Gold Line
description: Dark, intimate bar with a big vinyl collection offering craft cocktails amid chic '70s & '80s decor.
address: 5607 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Lucky Tiki Highland Park
description: When you arrive, look to your left for your old sailing buddy, and play a little shell game for entry.
address: 5621 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Los Nogales Nursery
description: Our 22-acre Sun Valley site is home to a retail nursery, demonstration gardens, an education center, gift shop, and hiking trail. Admission is free
address: 4700 N Griffin Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90031

name: Solarc Brewing
description: beer + tea + music glassell park~fermented art beverages and experiences
address: 3505 Eagle Rock Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90065

name: Cali Vibes Records
description: Los Angeles Punk and Reggae Music Chapel

name: Hermosillo Club
description: Hip, artsy haunt featuring rotating draft beers, wine & creative spins on global bar bites.

name: Common space brewery
description: Common Space Brewery is opening a new Highland Park taproom in 2026! Fresh beer, great food, Dodgers games, trivia nights, and family-friendly vibes await.

name: Ggiata Highland Park
description: Familiar counter-serve joint for Italian sandwiches and salads, plus classic dishes like chicken parm and arancini.

name: Moryork
description: An incredible gallery with an eclectic and high quality selection. It hosts cool live music events with its good acoustics and nice decor.

name: Belle's Delicatessen & Bar
description: Eastside spot making its own bagel sandwiches, along with homemade spreads, cookies and more.

name: Baller Hardware
description: Co-op chain selling home-improvement products & building materials, including lumber, tools & paint. This is a hidden gem plant shop tucked into the outdoor space at the rear of the hardware store just past a beautiful tile garden mosaic entryway.

name: Joy
description: Taiwanese classics get a hip twist at this Highland Park eatery from the Pine & Crane team.

name: Highland cafe
description: Laid-back coffee shop serving inventive egg dishes, sandwiches & salads in a warm brick interior.

name: Fig Earth Supply
description: Organic, heirloom nursery featuring a rotating stock of edible flowers and starter herb plants.
address: 3565 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90065

name: Osteria Mozza
description: Lauded upscale Italian cuisine from chef Nancy Silverton in a bustling atmosphere.

name: Jame Enoteca
description: Intimate eatery serving Italian dishes made from local ingredients, including hand-rolled pastas.

name: Bread, Espresso &
description: Relaxed chain for fluffy baked goods (milk bread, sandwiches and more), plus toast dishes and coffee.
address: 1212 S Pacific Coast Hwy Suite 100, Redondo Beach, CA 90277

name: Jus' Poke
description: Small restaurant serving a limited menu of Hawaiian specialties in a no-frills setting.
address: 501 N Pacific Coast Hwy, Redondo Beach, CA 90277

name: Esperanza Cocina de la Playa
description: Sonoran fare and handcrafted cocktails offered in a chic restaurant with global decor.
address: 309 Manhattan Beach Blvd, Manhattan Beach, CA 90266

name: The Slice & Pint
description: Easygoing restaurant with outdoor seating whipping up classic pizza, meatballs & pasta dishes.
address: 130 W Grand Ave, El Segundo, CA 90245

name: AttaGirl LA
description: Founded by Chefs Alice Mai and David LeFevre, AttaGirl brings a vibrant Mediterranean dining experience to Hermosa Beach

name: The Oaks Gourmet Market & Café
description: Small shop selling a range of wine, spirits, craft beers, upmarket groceries & prepared food.
address: 1915 N Bronson Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90068

name: Beethoven Market
description: Historic neighborhood market with handmade pastas, pizzas, rotisserie chicken, wine & cocktails.

name: Baroo
description: Hip, compact joint in a strip-mall preparing locally sourced Korean classics with a modern twist.

name: Sushi Sonagi
description: Daniel Son’s 8-seat Omakase sushi restaurant celebrating Japanese, Korean, and California traditions.

name: Bavel
description: Ori Menashe’s Middle Eastern follow-up to Bestia with small plates, meat, fish and desserts.

name: HiPPO
description: Contemporary American & Italian fare from an open kitchen with wine & cocktails.
address: 5916 1/2 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Farmshop
description: Innovative seasonal plates & creative cocktails delivered in rustic-chic quarters.

name: Playa Provisions
description: Beachside purveyor of ocean-goer treats including sandwiches, ice cream cones & cocktails.
address: 119 Culver Blvd, Playa Del Rey, CA 90293

name: Jyan Isaac Bread
description: Small, family-run bakery for a variety of sourdough loaves and bagels.
address: 1620 Ocean Park Blvd, Santa Monica, CA 90405

name: Evil Cooks
description: Casual venue for creative tacos including vegan options, and some served in bags, plus dessert.
address: 3333 N Eastern Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90032

name: Las Perlas
description: Stylish Mexican hot spot for mezcal, tequila & cocktails.

name: Villa's Tacos Los Angeles
description: Informal, line-prone eatery serving traditional tacos and agua frescas.
address: 5455 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Prince Street Pizza - Costa Mesa
description: Takeout pizzeria serving pan, regular and Sicilian-style slices.

name: Fishing with Dynamite
description: Oyster-focused seafood cafe with classic & creative eats.

name: Gjusta
description: Artisanal bakery in a sprawling space offering special breads baked on-site.

name: Burritos La Palma - Santa Ana
description: Basic counter-serve nook with burritos & tortas.
address: 410 N Bristol St Unit D, Santa Ana, CA 92703

name: Apollonia's Pizzeria
description: Easygoing parlor offering thin-crust specialty pies with playful names.

name: Providence
description: Chef Michael Cimarusti's fine-dining destination delivering innovative seafood.

name: Camphor
description: French recipes at this modern option for snacks, steak and cocktails.
address: 923 E 3rd St Suite 109, Los Angeles, CA 90013

name: Sonoratown - Long Beach
description: Offering homemade tacos and more in the traditional Sonora style.

name: Quarter Sheets
description: Detroit-style pies, focaccia, and sheet cakes.

name: Highly Likely
description: Though the interior is comfy and stylish, it’s all about the patio at Highly Likely in Highland Park. The stunning restaurant fills up quickly, especially the outdoor bar that serves frozen daiquiris on weekends and a cocktail menu at all other times.
address: 5526 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Zebulon
description: You Can either hang in the front or see a show in the back.

name: Brodard Restaurant
description: Modern Vietnamese cuisine with Asian-fusion touches & specialty cocktails served in a sleek space.

name: BIG ANT'S BBQ
description: No-frills spot for BBQ favorites such as brisket, ribs, and pulled pork, plus sides and desserts.
address: 2207 N San Fernando Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90065

name: Picaresca Barra de Cafe
description: Trendy coffee shop popular for breakfast and quick bites, including burritos, and chilaquiles.

name: Griffith Observatory & Park
description: The most democratic view in Los Angeles — the Griffith Observatory sits on the south slope of Mount Hollywood with a free 360° panorama of the city, the Hollywood Sign, and the Pacific on clear days. The park itself covers 4,310 acres of chaparral with miles of hiking trails. Go at sunset; stay for the star shows in the Samuel Oschin Planetarium.
address: 2800 E Observatory Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90027

name: The Getty Center
description: Richard Meier's travertine campus on a Brentwood hilltop — one of the great art museums in the world, free to enter (parking fee), with an extraordinary collection of European paintings, decorative arts, and photography alongside one of the finest garden designs in Southern California. The views of the city and the Pacific are as good as the art. Go on a Tuesday morning.
address: 1200 Getty Center Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90049

name: The Getty Villa
description: A replica of a Roman villa on the Malibu coast housing one of the world's finest collections of Greek, Roman, and Etruscan antiquities — 44,000 objects spanning 6,500 years. Less visited than the Getty Center and more intimate; the gardens, peristyle, and ocean setting make it unlike any other museum experience in LA. Tickets required; free but must be reserved online.
address: 17985 Pacific Coast Hwy, Pacific Palisades, CA 90272

name: LACMA
description: The largest art museum in the western United States — 150,000 objects spanning 6,000 years across 13 buildings on Wilshire Boulevard. The Urban Light installation (202 restored antique streetlamps) out front is LA's most photographed artwork. The Resnick Pavilion and the Broad Contemporary Art Museum within the campus are the strongest contemporary holdings.

name: The Broad
description: Eli and Edythe Broad's contemporary art museum on Grand Avenue in Downtown — a striking honeycomb-and-veil building housing 2,000 works by 200 artists including Jeff Koons, Cindy Sherman, Jean-Michel Basquiat, and Kara Walker. Free but timed entry tickets required. The Infinity Mirrored Room by Yayoi Kusama has its own separate ticket and sells out weeks in advance.
address: 221 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90012

name: Hammer Museum
description: UCLA's free contemporary art museum in Westwood — one of the most adventurous programming institutions in Los Angeles, with a focus on emerging and under-recognised artists. The Made in LA biennial is the most important survey of LA art practice. Free admission always. The bookshop and courtyard café are both excellent.

name: Museum of Jurassic Technology
description: The strangest museum in America — a Culver City institution that presents entirely fabricated natural history with the deadpan seriousness of a real museum. Exhibits on the Cameroon stink ant, trailer park trailer parks, and the history of mobile homes. David Hildebrand Wilson's creation has been confusing and delighting visitors since 1988. The rooftop tea room with the doves is real.

name: Huntington Library, Art Museum & Botanical Gardens
description: A 207-acre estate in San Marino housing one of the great research libraries in the world (Gutenberg Bible, Canterbury Tales manuscript), a strong art collection (Gainsborough's Blue Boy), and 16 distinct botanical gardens including the famous Japanese garden and the Desert Garden — the largest outdoor collection of cacti and succulents on earth. Best visited on a weekday morning.

name: The Last Bookstore
description: California's largest used and new bookstore, in a former bank vault in Downtown LA — two floors of books with a labyrinthine tunnel built from stacked paperbacks, a vinyl section, and an art gallery upstairs. The horror and sci-fi vault is a room unto itself. One of those bookshops that makes you forget you had somewhere else to be.
address: 453 S Spring St, Los Angeles, CA 90013

name: Grand Central Market
description: A Downtown LA institution since 1917 — a covered market on Broadway housing a mix of century-old stalls (Sarita's Pupuseria, China Café) alongside newer vendors (Eggslut, Wexler's Deli). The best place in the city to eat several things from several cuisines in one sitting. Busy on weekends; go on a weekday at 11am before the lunch rush.

name: Union Station
description: America's last great railway station, opened in 1939 — a stunning blend of Spanish Colonial Revival, Mission Revival, and Art Deco design with a soaring main hall, original leather seating, and a garden courtyard that has barely changed in 85 years. Still a functioning transit hub. Walk through even if you're not catching a train; the Mission-style waiting room is one of the great rooms in LA.
address: 800 N Alameda St, Los Angeles, CA 90012

name: Venice Canals
description: Six blocks of hand-dug canals built in 1905 by Abbot Kinney to evoke Venice, Italy — a quiet, residential neighbourhood of arched bridges, ducks, kayakers, and million-dollar bungalows just two blocks from the boardwalk chaos. Walk the canal path on a weekend morning for one of the most unexpectedly peaceful experiences in LA.
address: Venice Canal Historic District, Venice, CA 90291

name: Abbot Kinney Boulevard
description: Venice's main commercial strip — a mile of independent boutiques, restaurants, coffee shops, galleries, and design stores that captures what makes the LA creative economy feel distinct. Fridays bring food trucks. Best walked on a weekday when it's less crowded. The stretch between California and Westminster is the strongest.

name: Frogtown & the LA River
description: The most interesting neighbourhood in Los Angeles right now — a formerly industrial stretch along the concrete LA River between Atwater Village and Elysian Valley, now colonised by artists, breweries, coffee roasters, and small fabricators. Walk or bike the river path on a weekend; stop at Elysian Valley Arts Collective, Mable's Smokehouse, and the growing cluster of studios off Ripple Street.

name: Highland Park & York Boulevard
description: The Eastside neighbourhood that quietly became one of the most interesting in LA — York Boulevard lined with independent bookshops, excellent coffee, some of the city's best bars, and a longstanding Latino community that gives it genuine texture. The stretch between Figueroa and Avenue 50 is the core. Go on a Saturday evening.
address: York Blvd, Highland Park, Los Angeles, CA 90042

name: Boyle Heights & Mariachi Plaza
description: One of LA's most historically significant neighbourhoods — the heart of Mexican-American Los Angeles, home to Mariachi Plaza where musicians in full charro suits gather to be hired, the oldest operating bakery in the city (La Mascota), and Evergreen Cemetery where the city's early history is buried. A neighbourhood that rewards walking slowly.
address: Mariachi Plaza, Boyle Heights, Los Angeles, CA 90033

name: Leimert Park
description: The cultural heart of Black Los Angeles — a neighbourhood centred on Leimert Park Village with jazz clubs, African art galleries, record shops, and the Vision Theatre. The weekly drum circle on Sunday afternoons at the park bandshell has run for decades. Fifth Street Dick's and the World Stage anchor a music scene that is as important to LA as any in the city.
address: Leimert Park Village, Los Angeles, CA 90008

name: Echo Park
description: A neighbourhood and lake two miles northwest of Downtown — the lake itself is ringed with lotus plants that bloom spectacularly in late July, paddleboats for rent, and views of the Downtown skyline. The surrounding streets are dense with good coffee, used bookshops, and bars that have outlasted several waves of gentrification.
address: Echo Park Lake, Los Angeles, CA 90026

name: Silver Lake Reservoir Walk
description: The 2.5-mile walking path encircling the Silver Lake Reservoir — a neighbourhood institution where the city's creative class does its best thinking. The views of the hills, the water, and on clear mornings the San Gabriel Mountains make it one of the best urban walks in LA. Dog-friendly; coffee at Intelligentsia on Sunset is the standard finish.
address: Silver Lake Reservoir, Los Angeles, CA 90039

name: Runyon Canyon
description: 160 acres of chaparral park above Hollywood with three loop trails, panoramic city views, and the most famous dog-walking scene in Los Angeles. The upper trail ridge gives unobstructed views from Downtown to the Pacific. Go early morning on a weekday to avoid the crowds; the weekend scene at the Fuller Avenue entrance is its own spectacle.
address: 2000 N Fuller Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90046

name: Elysian Park
description: LA's oldest and second-largest park — 600 acres of hills above Dodger Stadium with eucalyptus groves, a lotus pond, a police academy rose garden open to the public, and miles of quiet trails almost never crowded. The views of the Downtown skyline from the upper trails are exceptional and almost entirely unknown to visitors.

name: Eaton Canyon
description: A Pasadena canyon trail that follows the creek bed 1.5 miles to a 40-foot waterfall — one of the most rewarding short hikes in the LA basin. The canyon cuts into the San Gabriel Mountains above the suburbs; the waterfall runs year-round. Busy on weekends; arrive before 8am to get parking and beat the crowds.

name: Malibu Creek State Park
description: 2,000 acres of rugged Santa Monica Mountains terrain — where M*A*S*H was filmed, where the creek runs through volcanic rock formations and pools, and where the Goat Buttes rise dramatically above the canyon floor. The Rock Pool trail is the main draw; the Backbone Trail access point for serious hikers. One of the finest state parks accessible from LA.

name: Point Dume
description: A headland jutting into the Pacific at the northern end of Santa Monica Bay — dramatic blufftop trails, a secluded beach below accessible by stairs, tidepools, and in winter the best land-based whale watching in Southern California. The view south from the point toward Malibu and the Santa Monica Mountains is the most beautiful coastal panorama in greater LA.

name: Topanga State Park
description: The largest wildland within the boundaries of a US city — 11,000 acres of Santa Monica Mountains chaparral between the San Fernando Valley and the Malibu coast, with 36 miles of trails and sweeping views of the Pacific. The Eagle Rock and Eagle Springs loop is the classic hike. Trailhead parking fills by 9am on weekends.

name: Crystal Cove State Park
description: Three and a half miles of undeveloped Pacific coastline in Newport Beach — tide pools, sea caves, a historic 1930s beach colony of cottages available to rent, and offshore diving. The coastal bluff trail above the beach offers some of the finest coastal walking in Southern California. The reef at Pelican Point is the best snorkelling within an hour of LA.
address: 8471 N Coast Hwy, Laguna Beach, CA 92651

name: Angeles Crest Highway
description: California State Route 2 climbing from La Cañada through the San Gabriel Mountains to Wrightwood — one of the great driving roads in Southern California, with pull-offs above the cloud line, access to serious high-country hiking, and in winter, snow less than an hour from Downtown LA. Mt. Wilson Observatory is the key stop; the views of the LA basin from 5,700 feet are extraordinary.
address: Angeles Crest Hwy (CA-2), La Cañada Flintridge, CA 91011

name: Watts Towers
description: Simon Rodia's life work — 17 interconnected sculptural towers rising up to 99 feet, built by one man over 33 years (1921–1954) using steel rods, wire mesh, and mortar embedded with over 70,000 pieces of glass, shells, tile, and pottery. One of the great works of outsider art anywhere in the world, in a South LA neighbourhood that most visitors never reach. Tours run on weekends.

name: Olvera Street & El Pueblo
description: The oldest part of Los Angeles — a Mexican marketplace on the site of the original pueblo, established 1781, with traditional craft stalls, a 1930s mural by David Alfaro Siqueiros (América Tropical, restored and viewable from an interpretive centre above), and the oldest surviving building in the city. The tamale and churro vendors have been here for decades. Free to walk.

name: The Original Farmers Market
description: Open since 1934 at 3rd and Fairfax — an open-air market of independent food stalls, butchers, produce vendors, and restaurants that has never stopped operating. Not to be confused with the weekend farmers markets elsewhere; this is a permanent institution. Du-par's for pancakes, Huntington Meats for the burger, and Monsieur Marcel for cheese and wine are the anchors.

name: Melrose Trading Post
description: LA's best flea market — every Sunday in the Fairfax High School parking lot, with 200+ vendors selling vintage clothing, furniture, records, ceramics, and art from the quality end of the flea market spectrum. The food trucks and the people-watching are excellent. Go before noon; the best stalls thin out by early afternoon.

name: Rose Bowl Flea Market
description: The largest flea market in California — held the second Sunday of every month in the Rose Bowl stadium parking lot in Pasadena. 2,500 vendors across 10 acres selling antiques, vintage clothing, mid-century furniture, art, and every conceivable category of collectible. Early entry (7am) costs more but gets you first access to the serious dealers. Cash-heavy.

name: MOCA
description: The Museum of Contemporary Art on Grand Avenue — the permanent collection is exceptional (Rothko, Pollock, Kline, Basquiat, Ruscha) and the programming consistently serious. The Geffen Contemporary in Little Tokyo is MOCA's second campus with better space for large-scale installation work. Together they form the strongest contemporary art institution in LA.
address: 250 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90012

name: Bergamot Station
description: A former trolley terminus in Santa Monica converted into a campus of art galleries — around 30 galleries in converted industrial buildings, anchored by the Santa Monica Museum of Art and the best concentration of serious contemporary art spaces in the city. Most galleries are free to enter. Gallery nights on the first Friday of each month draw the biggest crowds.

name: Arts District
description: Downtown LA's creative neighbourhood east of the 101 — converted warehouses and industrial buildings housing restaurants, breweries, galleries, and design studios that have made this the most architecturally and creatively active district in the city. Hauser & Wirth, Bestia, Bavel, and Angel City Brewery are the anchors. Walk the grid between Traction and 6th Street.

name: Koreatown
description: The densest neighbourhood in Los Angeles and one of the largest Koreatowns outside Korea — a 24-hour district of Korean BBQ restaurants, karaoke rooms, spas (the jjimjilbang at Wi Spa is essential), nightclubs, and strip malls hiding some of the best food in the city. The action runs until 4am on weekends. Olympic Boulevard is the main artery; Normandie is the western anchor.

name: Hollywood Sign
description: The most recognizable landmark in Los Angeles — nine 45-foot letters on Mount Lee in the Santa Monica Mountains. The sign is fenced off but the hikes are the point: Hollyridge Trail and the trail from Griffith Observatory both deliver strong views. Lake Hollywood Park on the valley side gives the classic photo angle. No parking near the sign — arrive early or take a rideshare.
address: Mount Lee, Griffith Park, Los Angeles, CA 90028

name: Santa Monica Pier
description: The western terminus of Route 66 and the oldest operating pier on the West Coast. Pacific Park at the end features a solar-powered Ferris wheel that has become an LA icon. The pier is free to walk; the aquarium underneath is worth a look. Best at sunset — the light over the Pacific is legitimately spectacular.
address: 200 Santa Monica Pier, Santa Monica, CA 90401

name: Walt Disney Concert Hall
description: Frank Gehry's stainless steel masterpiece on Bunker Hill — one of the most acoustically refined concert halls ever built and the home of the LA Philharmonic. The exterior is worth a visit on its own; the self-guided interior tour is free on non-performance days. The rooftop garden is open to the public. Seeing the Phil perform here is a genuine LA bucket-list experience.
address: 111 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90012

name: TCL Chinese Theatre
description: The most famous movie palace in the world — opened in 1927 and still operating as a first-run IMAX cinema. The forecourt cement blocks with celebrity handprints and footprints are the draw: Marilyn Monroe, Bogart, Clint Eastwood, all cast in concrete. The VIP tour goes inside the main auditorium, which is genuinely stunning. Go early before the bus tour crowds arrive.

name: Hollywood Walk of Fame
description: 2.5 miles of sidewalk embedded with over 2,700 brass-and-terrazzo stars honoring film, TV, music, radio, and theatre legends. The stars run along Hollywood Boulevard and Vine Street. Free to walk at any hour. Treat it as a neighborhood to explore rather than a destination — the Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel and Musso & Frank Grill are both nearby and worth stopping at.

name: The Grove
description: An outdoor shopping complex adjacent to the Original Farmers Market — upscale retail, restaurants, and an elaborate fountain show. Connected directly to the historic Farmers Market at the north end, where you should eat. Anchor stores include Nordstrom and Apple. The whole thing works as an experience even if the retail is predictable.
address: 189 The Grove Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90036

name: Rodeo Drive
description: Three blocks of luxury retail in Beverly Hills — Chanel, Gucci, Cartier, Hermès, Tiffany on one stretch. The window-shopping and people-watching are genuinely entertaining even if you're not buying. The Two Rodeo pedestrian arcade is worth a detour. Best on a weekday morning before the tour bus crowds arrive.

name: Melrose Avenue
description: The creative spine of central LA — the stretch between Crescent Heights and Highland is the core: vintage shops, streetwear boutiques, tattoo studios, and some of LA's best restaurants. Paul Smith's pink wall is here. Maxfield is the definitive LA luxury concept store. The strip from La Brea east toward Melrose Hill is the newer, more neighbourhood edge.

name: Westfield Century City
description: The best pure shopping mall in LA — fully reimagined in 2017 with an open-air design and extensive dining. Anchored by Nordstrom and Macy's with over 200 stores. Eataly is worth visiting on its own. The AMC dine-in cinema is on the upper level. Easy freeway access makes it the default for westside shopping.
address: 10250 Santa Monica Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90067

name: Bradbury Building
description: The most extraordinary interior in Los Angeles — a five-story Victorian commercial building from 1893 with an open atrium, ornate wrought-iron balconies, glazed yellow brick, and a glass roof that floods the space with light. Used as a film location in Blade Runner, Chinatown, and dozens of others. The lobby is open to the public during business hours. One of the great architectural experiences in America.

name: Bullocks Wilshire Building
description: The finest Art Deco building in Los Angeles — a 1929 department store with one of the most beautiful facades in the city. The copper patina tower and zigzag ornamentation are worth the detour. Now Southwestern Law School's library; the interior is accessible by appointment. The porte-cochère on the back, designed for customers arriving by car, is an extraordinary period detail.

name: Eastern Columbia Building
description: A 1930 Art Deco-Gothic tower clad entirely in turquoise terracotta with gold ornamentation — the most vivid building on the Broadway Historic Theatre District stretch. Designed by Claude Beelman; now converted to residential lofts. Best viewed from the corner of Broadway and 9th Street.

name: Ennis House
description: Frank Lloyd Wright's most dramatic Los Angeles building — a 1924 textile-block house in Los Feliz that reads more like a Mayan temple than a residence. Used as a film location in Blade Runner, The House on Haunted Hill, and Twin Peaks: The Return. Tours are offered periodically by the Trust for Preservation of Cultural Heritage.

name: Hollyhock House
description: Frank Lloyd Wright's first Los Angeles commission — completed in 1921 for oil heiress Aline Barnsdall on the hilltop park that now bears her name. The abstracted hollyhock motif runs through the entire building, inside and out. Now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The park below offers one of the better views of the Hollywood Hills. Tours run Wednesday through Sunday.

name: Sheats-Goldstein Residence
description: John Lautner's 1963 masterpiece above Benedict Canyon — a concrete residence cascading down a hillside with a glass wall opening completely to the city below. Fashion designer James Goldstein owns it and has opened it for LACMA fundraiser events. The infinity pool and nightclub addition Lautner completed in the 1970s pushed the design even further. One of the great houses of the 20th century.
address: 10104 Angelo View Dr, Beverly Hills, CA 90210

name: Stahl House
description: Case Study House #22, designed by Pierre Koenig in 1959 — the glass-and-steel house cantilevering over the Hollywood Hills that Julius Shulman photographed into an icon of mid-century modern architecture. Tours are available by appointment; standing where that photo was taken is genuinely moving.

name: Schindler House
description: Rudolf Schindler's 1922 experimental residence in West Hollywood — one of the foundational works of California modernism, designed as a cooperative housing experiment with sleeping baskets on the roof. Now the MAK Center for Art and Architecture, with rotating contemporary exhibitions. The house itself, with its sliding doors, concrete and canvas construction, and outdoor sleeping porches, remains a genuinely radical object.
address: 835 N Kings Rd, West Hollywood, CA 90069

name: Theme Building
description: The iconic flying-saucer-on-stilts at the center of LAX — designed in 1961 by William Pereira and Charles Luckman as a symbol of the jet age. The Encounter Restaurant that occupied the upper level closed in 2013, but the exterior remains one of the great Googie architecture landmarks in Los Angeles. Visible from the airport roadway and parking structures.

name: Griffith Park
description: The largest urban park in the United States — 4,310 acres of chaparral-covered hills above Los Feliz and Silver Lake. The Griffith Observatory sits at its center and is the best viewpoint in the city. The park also contains the LA Zoo, the Autry Museum, Travel Town, two golf courses, the Greek Theatre, and 53 miles of hiking trails. The trail to the Hollywood Sign starts here.
address: 4730 Crystal Springs Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90027

name: Angels Flight Railway
description: The world's shortest railway — two funicular cars named Sinai and Olivet that have climbed 298 feet of Bunker Hill since 1901. The ride takes about a minute and costs $1. At the top is Grand Central Market and the rest of Bunker Hill; at the bottom is Hill Street and the old financial district. Small, historic, and genuinely charming.
address: 350 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90071

name: Natural History Museum of LA
description: One of the largest natural history museums in the world — housed in a 1913 Spanish Renaissance building in Exposition Park. The dinosaur hall is exceptional, with some of the most complete T. rex specimens ever found displayed together. The gem and mineral vault and the North American mammals diorama are both worth time. Easy to combine with the California Science Center next door.

name: California Science Center
description: Home to Space Shuttle Endeavour — the only shuttle displayed in launch-ready vertical configuration, which makes the scale viscerally clear in a way horizontal displays cannot. General admission is free; the IMAX theatre and shuttle experience cost extra. Exposition Park location puts it adjacent to the Natural History Museum.
address: 700 Exposition Park Dr, Los Angeles, CA 90037

name: Academy Museum of Motion Pictures
description: The definitive museum of cinema — opened in 2021 in the restored Saban Building on Wilshire. The permanent collection covers the full history of filmmaking with extraordinary artifacts: Dorothy's ruby slippers, the Alien creature, R2-D2, original Citizen Kane equipment. The glass sphere addition houses the 1,000-seat David Geffen Theatre and a rooftop terrace with views across the Miracle Mile. Get tickets in advance.

name: Petersen Automotive Museum
description: The world's largest automotive museum — 300,000 square feet on Wilshire, redesigned in 2015 with a striking stainless steel ribbon facade. The collection runs from early horseless carriages through Formula 1 cars to movie vehicles and concept cars that never went into production. The Vault downstairs, accessible only on guided tours, houses several hundred additional vehicles. Adjacent to LACMA and the Academy Museum.

name: Skirball Cultural Center
description: A Jewish cultural museum and arts center in the hills above the 405 in Bel Air — designed by Moshe Safdie and opened in 1996. The permanent Visions and Values exhibition traces Jewish history from ancient Israel through the immigrant experience in America. Noah's Ark is the beloved hands-on children's exhibit. Free parking; campus is beautiful.
address: 2701 N Sepulveda Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90049

name: Autry Museum of the American West
description: The most comprehensive museum of Western American history in the country — founded by singing cowboy Gene Autry in 1988 at the edge of Griffith Park. The permanent collection of 500,000+ objects covers Native American art, the Gold Rush, cattle ranching, and the mythology of the West through film and TV. Smart, serious curating and consistently strong temporary exhibitions.
address: 4700 Western Heritage Way, Los Angeles, CA 90027

name: Japanese American National Museum
description: The only museum in the country dedicated to the Japanese American experience — in the heart of Little Tokyo on First Street. The permanent exhibition on the internment of Japanese Americans during WWII is one of the most important and carefully curated in Los Angeles. Free Thursdays from 5–8pm and all day on the third Thursday of each month.
address: 100 N Central Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90012

name: Grammy Museum
description: Four floors of music history at the heart of LA Live — interactive exhibits on the recording process, genre history, and the Grammy legacy. The collection includes handwritten lyrics, costumes, and instruments across every genre. The interactive sound labs let you mix and record. Small enough to do thoroughly in two to three hours.
address: 800 W Olympic Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90015

name: La Brea Tar Pits & Museum
description: The only active Ice Age fossil excavation site in an urban area — pools of natural asphalt have been trapping and preserving animals for 50,000 years, and paleontologists are still digging. The outdoor pits with their bubble-surfacing tar are genuinely eerie; the museum houses more than three million fossils, including complete mammoth and saber-toothed cat skeletons. Project 23 lets visitors watch active excavation in progress. Next door to LACMA.

name: Beverly Center
description: An eight-level enclosed mall on the corner of Beverly and La Cienega — distinctive for its exterior perimeter escalators and the brutalist parking structure that wraps the building. The 2018 renovation updated the interior and brought in better dining options. The location puts it equidistant from Beverly Hills, West Hollywood, and the Fairfax corridor.

name: Americana at Brand
description: A lifestyle shopping center in Glendale modeled loosely on the Grove — outdoor walkways, a dancing fountain, and a trolley connecting shops to parking. The retail mix is stronger than the Grove's and less touristed. Connected to the Glendale Galleria by a skybridge. Worth the drive when combined with something else in the area.

name: Third Street Promenade
description: A three-block pedestrian street in Santa Monica running from Wilshire to Broadway — outdoor cafés, street performers, bookstores, chain retail, and a movie theatre. The energy is consistent from morning to midnight. A block from the beach and two blocks from the Pier. The more interesting restaurants are on the streets immediately adjacent rather than on the Promenade itself.

name: Sunset Strip
description: The 1.5-mile stretch of Sunset Boulevard through West Hollywood where the city's entertainment industry has concentrated since the 1920s — the Chateau Marmont, Comedy Store, Troubadour, the Roxy, and Whisky a Go Go all sit here. The billboards are massive and constantly rotating. The sidewalk scene is best on weeknights when the after-work crowd mixes with the industry dinner crowd.

name: ROW DTLA
description: A 32-acre adaptive reuse complex in the Arts District — former industrial and warehouse buildings converted into restaurants, retail, offices, and event spaces. The Market Hall anchors the retail side with independent food vendors. Smorgasburg LA sets up here on Sundays. Several of the best new restaurants in DTLA have opened here. Good parking and a natural anchor for exploring the broader Arts District.
address: 777 S Alameda St, Los Angeles, CA 90021

name: Smorgasburg LA
description: LA's biggest outdoor food market — every Sunday at Row DTLA from 10am to 4pm, with 80+ vendors covering every cuisine, style, and price point. The Brooklyn-based market exported well to LA; the vendor quality is consistently high and the rotation keeps it fresh. Hits have launched brick-and-mortar restaurants across the city. Cash is accepted but cards are easier.
address: 777 S Alameda St, Los Angeles, CA 90021

name: Silver Lake Flea
description: A curated monthly flea market in Silver Lake with a vintage and independent maker focus — clothing, furniture, ceramics, jewelry, and records from vendors who know their stock. The neighborhood location means the surrounding coffee shops and restaurants are part of the trip. Check the website for current dates and location; it has moved within the neighborhood over the years.

name: Artists & Fleas Venice
description: A curated weekend marketplace near Abbot Kinney focused on independent designers, vintage sellers, and local artisans. The vendor selection skews toward handmade goods, vintage clothing, accessories, and art over junk-store resale. Pairs naturally with a walk down Abbot Kinney and lunch at one of the surrounding restaurants.

name: Unique Markets
description: A quarterly pop-up marketplace bringing together independent designers, vintage dealers, and makers across fashion, home goods, beauty, and food — typically held at the Barker Hangar in Santa Monica with 300+ vendors. The production value is high; it feels like a curated festival rather than a flea market. Flagship events happen in spring and fall; check the website for current dates.

name: Atwater Village Farmers Market
description: A neighborhood-scale Sunday farmers market in Atwater Village — smaller and calmer than the Hollywood or Silver Lake markets, with a loyal local following. Strong produce vendors, a handful of prepared food stalls, and the relaxed vibe that's harder to find at the bigger markets. Runs Sundays from 10am to 2pm. The surrounding Glendale Boulevard strip has good coffee nearby.

name: Hollywood Farmers Market
description: One of the largest and most popular farmers markets in Los Angeles — held every Sunday on Ivar and Selma Avenues from 8am to 1pm. Strong year-round produce from California farms, good prepared food options, and a reliable mix of artisan vendors. The Hollywood location means parking is the main challenge; walking or rideshare is advisable.
address: Ivar Ave & Selma Ave, Hollywood, CA 90028

name: Hollywood Bowl
description: The greatest outdoor venue in America — a 17,500-seat natural amphitheater in the Hollywood Hills that has hosted Billie Holiday, the Beatles, Frank Sinatra, and the LA Phil under the same shell since 1922. Bring a picnic; box seats allow full picnic spreads with wine. The pre-show tailgate in the parking lot is as much the experience as the show itself.
address: 2301 N Highland Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90068

name: Greek Theatre
description: A 5,870-seat outdoor amphitheater inside Griffith Park — a mid-sized venue that gets acts too big for club shows but not quite stadium-scale. The sight lines are excellent from almost every seat; the natural hillside setting makes every show feel special. Walk in from the Vermont Canyon parking lot on summer evenings when the light through the trees is perfect.
address: 2700 N Vermont Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90027

name: El Capitan Theatre
description: Disney's Hollywood flagship cinema — a 1926 movie palace on Hollywood Boulevard fully restored to its original Churrigueresque splendor. Disney premieres and exclusive engagements anchor the programming; the theatre occasionally features live performances before screenings. The organ that rises from the stage pit is a genuine period detail.

name: Dolby Theatre
description: Home of the Academy Awards since 2002 — a 3,400-seat theatre at Hollywood & Highland designed to host the Oscars. The rest of the year it hosts touring Broadway shows, concerts, and special events. Tours run daily and include the press room and the VIP room where Oscar winners are photographed.

name: The Wiltern
description: A 1930 Art Deco theatre on the corner of Wilshire and Western in Koreatown — one of the most beautiful music venues in Los Angeles. The terra cotta turquoise exterior is matched by a stunning Zigzag Moderne interior with original light fixtures and murals. Capacity of about 2,300, the right size for acts between the intimate club stage and the arena. Good sound, excellent sight lines, and the surrounding Korean restaurants are the ideal pre-show dinner.

name: Troubadour
description: The most historically significant small venue in Los Angeles — opened in 1957 on Santa Monica Boulevard, where Elton John, James Taylor, the Eagles, Tom Waits, and Joni Mitchell all played early career-defining shows. Capacity of 500 keeps it intimate. The balcony sightlines are perfect; the floor gets packed. Acts here are on their way up or returning to their roots.
address: 9081 Santa Monica Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90069

name: Comedy Store
description: The most important comedy club in America — on the Sunset Strip since 1972, where Richard Pryor, Robin Williams, Jim Carrey, and Chris Rock all developed their acts. Three rooms: the Original Room for established comedians, the Main Room for headliners, and the Belly Room for experimental sets. Mitzi Shore's family still runs it; the history here is thick.

name: Largo at the Coronet
description: LA's most beloved intimate performing arts venue — a 300-seat theatre on La Cienega in West Hollywood with a curatorial sensibility that bridges comedy, music, and performance art. Jon Brion's Friday night residencies are the defining Largo experience: semi-improvised performances where he loops instruments, brings out surprise guests, and plays for three hours. No late seating; the crowd takes the work seriously.
address: 366 N La Cienega Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90048

name: Dynasty Typewriter
description: A 250-seat independent performance space near MacArthur Park — one of LA's most adventurous comedy and variety venues, programming experimental stand-up, one-person shows, panel talks, and genre-defying performance that doesn't fit anywhere else in the city. The booking taste runs toward comedians who write rather than just perform.

name: Crypto.com Arena
description: The home of the Lakers, Clippers, Kings, and Sparks — a 20,000-seat arena at LA Live that has hosted the Grammy Awards and some of the most significant sporting events in city history. Formerly the Staples Center, renamed in 2021. The Lakers and Clippers alternate on the same floor. Floor seats at a Lakers game are a legitimate LA bucket-list experience.
address: 1111 S Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90015

name: Dodger Stadium
description: The most beautiful ballpark in baseball — opened in 1962 in Chavez Ravine and meticulously maintained, with sightlines to the San Gabriel Mountains that are unmatched in the sport. The Dodger Dog is a cultural institution; the organ and crowd atmosphere are old school in the best way. Take the Dodger Stadium Express from Union Station and avoid the parking lot entirely.
address: 1000 Vin Scully Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90012

name: SoFi Stadium
description: The most expensive stadium ever built — $5.5 billion, opened in 2020 in Inglewood as home to the Rams and Chargers. The translucent roof covers the seating bowl while leaving the field open to the air; the giant oval videoboard is the most elaborate in any stadium. Site of Super Bowl LVI and the 2028 Olympic Opening Ceremony. The Inglewood Line light rail connects it without a car.

name: Universal Studios Hollywood
description: A working film studio and theme park combined — the studio tour tram ride through active backlot sets is the unique proposition here. The Wizarding World of Harry Potter is the headline theme park element. Tickets are expensive; the Express Pass is worth it to avoid waits. Best on weekdays; summer weekends are extremely crowded.
address: 100 Universal City Plaza, Universal City, CA 91608

name: Warner Bros. Studio Tour Hollywood
description: The most serious studio tour in Hollywood — a three-hour walking and cart tour through active Burbank production facilities including the ER set, the Central Perk café from Friends, and the DC and Harry Potter prop collections. Unlike Universal, this is an actual working studio; you'll frequently encounter active productions. The Stage 48 exhibit on the craft of filmmaking is legitimately educational.

name: Paramount Pictures Studio Tour
description: The only major studio tour still operating in Hollywood proper — inside the Paramount lot on Melrose Avenue, covering 110 acres of active production facilities including current TV and film sets, the wardrobe department, and the prop archive. The oldest continuously operating movie studio in Hollywood. Book well in advance; tours sell out.

name: Sony Pictures Studios
description: The former MGM lot in Culver City — where The Wizard of Oz, Singin' in the Rain, and Gone with the Wind were made. The two-hour walking tour covers the soundstages, backlot streets, the studio's prop collection, and the Wheel of Fortune and Jeopardy sets. Tours run Monday through Friday and must be booked in advance.
address: 10202 W Washington Blvd, Culver City, CA 90232

name: Raleigh Studios Hollywood
description: One of Hollywood's oldest independent studios — operating since 1914 on the same Melrose Avenue lot where early silent films were made. Chinatown, Titanic pre-production, and dozens of major TV productions have been made here. Operates exclusively as a working facility with no public tours; the exterior and gate are worth noting for their longevity in an industry that rarely preserves its own history.

name: Sunset Gower Studios
description: A historic Hollywood production facility at Sunset and Gower — on the site of the original Columbia Pictures studio Harry Cohn built in the 1920s. Now an independent facility housing major Netflix productions and streaming series. No public tours. The Sunset and Gower location is historically known as Gower Gulch, the old B-western studio district.

name: CBS Television City
description: The purpose-built broadcast television facility CBS constructed in 1952 — home to The Price is Right, Let's Make a Deal, and decades of live television production. Free tickets to tapings of game shows and talk programs are available through the CBS audience website. A significant example of mid-century modernist industrial design; reportedly slated for eventual redevelopment.

name: Fox Studio Lot
description: The Fox lot in Century City — where M*A*S*H, Titanic, Planet of the Apes, and Avatar were made. No public tours are offered; the studio operates as a secure working facility. The New York Street backlot exterior is visible from Pico Boulevard. The Disney acquisition of Fox's film assets in 2019 has shifted its production portfolio significantly.
address: 10201 W Pico Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90064

name: Hollywood Forever Cemetery
description: The most storied cemetery in Hollywood — established in 1899 and the final resting place of Rudolph Valentino, Cecil B. DeMille, Jayne Mansfield, and Johnny Ramone. The grounds are maintained as a public park and are genuinely beautiful. Cinespia outdoor film screenings on the Fairbanks Lawn during summer are a quintessential LA experience — bring a blanket, a bottle of wine, and arrive early for the best spot on the grass.

name: The Magic Castle
description: A private members' club for the Academy of Magical Arts in a Victorian mansion above Hollywood Boulevard — arguably the best magic venue in the world. Three performance rooms run simultaneously with close-up and stage magic from the finest working magicians on the circuit. Access requires membership or a member's invitation; the easiest visitor route is booking a room at the adjacent Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel, which provides guest passes. Dress code is strictly enforced.
